1: The funeral song I take up over you — qinah asher ani nose' 'aleykhem: qinah is the Hebrew funeral lament, characterized by a distinctive 3+2 beat meter (qinah meter) — three stressed syllables followed by two, mimicking the stumbling gait of grief. Amos is singing Israel's funeral before the death occurs — the prophetic perfect expressing certainty of coming events as already accomplished. The form is as striking as the content: the living are addressed in their own lament-song.
2: She has fallen, never to rise again — nafela lo-tosif qum betulat Yisra'el: betulah (virgin/maiden) as a personification of the nation emphasizes both youth and vulnerability; a young woman who should have a future. Natsha 'al-admatah eyn meqimah (abandoned on her own land, with no one to lift her up) — natash (forsaken, abandoned) + ein meqimah (no one to raise her) — two absences: no rescuer, no restoration. The qinah meter can be felt in the Hebrew rhythm.
3: The city that sent out a thousand will have a hundred survivors — ha'ir hayotse'et elef tashir me'ah: a devastating attrition ratio: 90% losses. A city that fielded a thousand soldiers will return a hundred; a hundred will return ten. Le-vet Yisra'el (to the house of Israel) — the remnant calculation is made in relationship to Israel's national structure. The mathematical precision is rhetorical: the losses are absolute and calculable.
4: Seek me and live — dorshuni vi-cheyu: the pivot from doom to invitation. Darash (seek, inquire, resort to) is the technical term for seeking an oracle at a sanctuary (2 Kings 1:2; Amos 22:18) but also for genuine turning to YHWH. The command is brief and total: seek me, not the shrine. Vi-cheyu (and live) — the covenant life-or-death formula (Deuteronomy 30:19-20): seeking YHWH produces life; the alternative is death.
5: Do not seek Bethel; do not go to Gilgal — the three apostate pilgrimage sites: Vet-El (Bethel), ha-Gilgal (Gilgal), Be'er-Sheva (Beersheba). Bethel and Dan had golden calves set up by Jeroboam I (1 Kings 12:29); Gilgal had degenerated from its covenant-renewal history; Beersheba in Judah had also become a pilgrimage site. Ki ha-Gilgal galoh yigle (for Gilgal will certainly go into exile) — wordplay: Gilgal-galoh-yigle (the place-name, the infinitive absolute, the imperfect) creates a triple sound-resonance. Ve-Vet-El yihyeh le-aven (Bethel will be reduced to nothing/nothingness) — aven (nothingness/wickedness) is a pun on Bethel (house of God) becoming Beth-Aven (house of nothing); cf. Hosea 4:15; Amos 10:5.
6: Seek the LORD and live — dirshu et-YHWH vi-cheyu: the imperative repeated with YHWH as the direct object, making explicit what v4 implied. Pen yitslach ke-esh vet Yosef (or he will sweep through the house of Joseph like a fire) — Joseph = the northern kingdom (Ephraim/Manasseh). The fire image from the oracle-series returns; now YHWH himself is the consuming fire if seeking is refused. Ve-akhlah ve-eyn mekhabbeh le-Vet-El (devouring it with no one to put it out at Bethel) — the shrine that was supposed to be the fire of sacred offering becomes the target of consuming judgment.
7: You who twist justice into bitter poison — ha-hofkhim la-la'anah mishpat: la'anah (wormwood, absinth) — a bitter plant (Deuteronomy 29:17; Jeremiah 9:14; Revelation 8:11) used as a metaphor for the bitterness of perverted justice. Ve-tsedaqah la-arets hinihu (and hurl righteousness to the ground) — hinniach (cause to rest, lay down) used here with violent force: righteousness is thrown down. The two covenant virtues — mishpat (justice) and tsedaqah (righteousness) — the same pair that appears in Amos 5:24 — are here both destroyed.
8: He who made the Pleiades and Orion — 'oseh Kimah ve-Kesil: the second doxological hymn-fragment (Amos 5:8-9). Kimah (Pleiades — the seven-star cluster) and Kesil (Orion — the giant constellation) are named in Job 9:9; Amos 38:31-32 as examples of divine creative power. Ve-hafokheth la-boqer tsalmaveth (who turns deep darkness into dawn) — tsalmaveth (shadow of death, deep darkness) reversed to boqer (morning/dawn): the cosmic reversal of darkness to light is YHWH's routine act. Yom layla hechshikh (and darkens the day into night) — the same power works in both directions. Ha-qore' le-me ha-yam (who summons the waters of the sea) — the flood/deluge theme; YHWH controls the waters that could overwhelm the earth.
9: He sends destruction flashing against the powerful — ha-mavlig shod al-az: balag (make flash/gleam; some read 'smile upon' or 'bring suddenly') — the connotation of lightning-speed destruction overtaking the strong. Ve-shod al-mibtsar yavo' (so that ruin comes upon the fortified city) — the armonot (fortresses) of the accusation are the target of the creator's power. The doxology grounds the judgment in creation-sovereignty: the God who made the stars and controls seas and darkness can certainly destroy human fortresses.
10: They hate the judge who rebukes them at the gate — sane'u va-sha'ar mokhiach: sha'ar (city gate) was the location of legal proceedings, commercial transactions, and civic life; the elder-judges who sat at the gate (shoftim ba-sha'ar) were the embodiment of public justice. Mokhiach (one who rebukes, corrects, adjudicates) — the proper judicial function hated. Ve-dover tamim yete'avuhu (and they despise the one who tells the truth/speaks with integrity) — tamim (blameless, complete, truthful) — honest witness was anathema to those who controlled justice by bribes.
11: You crush the poor and impose a grain tax — ya'an boshaskhem al-dal: boshaskhem from bas or bush — the precise sense is debated; 'trample, crush, press' captures it. U-mas bar tiqqechu mimmennu (you impose a grain tax on him) — mas bar (grain levy/rental) — demanding grain-rent from tenant farmers. The ironic reversal follows: batim gazit beniitem ve-lo teshvu bam (you have built houses of dressed stone but will not live in them) — gazit (hewn/dressed stone) indicates luxury construction; the covenant-curse of Deuteronomy 28:30 ('you will build a house but not live in it') is explicitly echoed.
12: I know how numerous your crimes are and how great your sins — ki yadati rabbim pish'eykhem: YHWH's direct first-person statement with yada' (know) — the covenant-knowing that began with Israel's election (Amos 3:2) now functions in judgment. Three charges in one verse: tsarrei tsaddiq (oppressors of the innocent/righteous), loquchei kofer (those who accept bribes — kofer = ransom, payment to avoid justice), and evyonim ba-sha'ar hittum (who deny justice to the needy at the gate) — the gate-justice system completely corrupted.
13: Therefore the wise man stays silent at a time like this — lachen hamaskil ba'et hahi yiddom: maskil (discerning person, wise one) — one who understands the situation. Yiddom (stays silent) — the root damam (be still, silent) used for stunned grief and prudential silence when speech is futile or dangerous. Ki et ra'ah hi (for it is an evil time) — the situation is so corrupted that even the wise person's counsel cannot be heard; speaking into a deaf system is futile. This verse anticipates the kind of contextual prudence taught in Ecclesiastes.
14: Seek good and not evil, that you may live — dirshu tov ve-al-ra' lema'an tich'yu: the seek-and-live formula from vv4-6 now applied to moral categories rather than cultic ones: seek tov (good) rather than YHWH vs. the apostate shrines. The two formulations together make the point: seeking YHWH and seeking good are inseparable. Vi-yehi khen YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot ittekhem (and then the LORD, the God of armies, will be with you) — the divine-with-you presence (immanuel) is the result of seeking the good; the conditional is striking: divine presence is connected to ethical seeking.
15: Hate evil and love good; establish justice at the gate — sinu ra' ve-'ahavu tov: the emotional verbs are as strong as possible — not merely choose or prefer but sane' (hate) evil and 'ahav (love) good. Ve-hatstsegu va-sha'ar mishpat (establish justice at the gate) — the gate-justice system, which was corrupted in v10, must be reformed. The result is tentative: ulai yechannen YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot she'erit Yosef (perhaps the LORD… will be gracious to the remnant of Joseph) — the ulai (perhaps) of divine sovereign freedom; even repentance does not compel YHWH. The remnant (she'erit) concept appears: there may be survivors, but it is YHWH's prerogative.
16: In all the public squares there will be wailing — be-khol-rechavot misped: rechovot (public squares, broad streets) and kol-chutsot (all the streets) — the mourning fills every public space. Qar'u ikkar el-eivel (farmers will be summoned to mourn) — ikkar (farmer, plowman) is called from agricultural work to the funeral; farming stops. Ve-eliqinah el-misped (and professional mourners to lead the lament) — yode'ei nehi (those skilled in lamentation) — the professional mourning-guilds whose wailing was hired for funerals; Israel is so comprehensively dead that even the professional mourners are insufficient.
17: Wailing in every vineyard — uve-khol-keramim misped: the vineyard (place of joy and harvest celebration) becomes a place of mourning. Ki-e'evor beqirbeka (for I will pass through your midst, declares the LORD) — the Passover vocabulary is unmistakable: 'avar (pass through) is the verb for YHWH's act in Exodus 12:12 ('I will pass through the land of Egypt'). The first Passover was death passing over Israel while striking Egypt; now YHWH passes through Israel bringing death. The protection of Passover is reversed for a faithless covenant people.
18: Woe to you who long for the day of the LORD — hoy ha-mit'avvim et yom YHWH: the first explicit treatment of yom YHWH (the Day of the LORD) in the writing prophets. The concept predates Amos (the people 'long for' it as an established expectation), but Amos inverts its popular meaning. Israel expected the Day to bring military victory for Israel against her enemies — a day of divine intervention on their behalf. Amos announces: lamah-zeh lakhem yom YHWH hu-choshek ve-lo-or (Why would you want the day of the LORD? It is darkness, not light) — the inversion is total. Those confident in covenant status will find judgment, not deliverance.
19: Like a man who fled from a lion and ran into a bear — ka'asher yanus ish mipnei ha-arye u-paga'o ha-dov: a vivid chain of escalating inescapability. Lion → bear → serpent: each escape from one danger leads directly into the next. U-va ha-vayit ve-samakh yado al-haqqir u-neshako ha-nachash (escaped into his house and put his hand on the wall only to be bitten by a snake) — even the domestic refuge is lethal. The three-threat chain illustrates that the Day of the LORD cannot be outrun or outmaneuvered by any human strategy.
20: Is not the day of the LORD darkness and not light — halo-choshek yom YHWH ve-lo-or: the rhetorical question demands agreement; the expected answer is 'yes, of course.' Ve-afelah ve-lo-naggah lo (pitch dark, with no gleam of brightness) — two synonyms for darkness: afelah (thick darkness) and the negation of naggah (gleam/brightness). The total elimination of light-expectation seals the inversion begun in v18.
21: I hate and despise your religious festivals — sane'ti me'asti chaggeykhem: the divine first-person rejection of the cult is stated with maximum emotional force: sane' (hate) + ma'as (despise, reject utterly). Chaggim (religious festivals, pilgrimage feasts) — the three prescribed pilgrimage feasts of Israel (Exodus 23:14-17; Lev 23). Ve-lo ariach be-'atsaroteykhem (I take no pleasure in your solemn gatherings) — 'atsarah (sacred assembly, solemn gathering) — the formal convocations are rejected. The rejection is of the form without substance: not of festival observance per se but of festival observance that coexists with injustice.
22: Even when you bring me burnt offerings and grain offerings — ki im-ta'alu li olot u-minchoteykhem: the three major offering categories: 'olah (burnt offering — completely burned), minchah (grain offering), and shlamim (fellowship/peace offerings — shared meal). All are rejected. Lo ertsah (I will not accept them) and lo abbit (I will not look at them) — two formulas of divine rejection: neither the offerings nor the one offering them receives divine attention. The cult has become a substitute for covenant relationship rather than its expression.
23: Away with the noise of your songs — hasir meiali hamon shireykhah: hamon (noise, tumult) applied to the songs — what should be worship has become loud performance. Ve-zimrat nevalekha lo eshma' (I will not listen to the music of your harps) — nevel (harp, lute) was the prestige instrument of Levitical worship; its rejection is the rejection of the entire musical apparatus of the cult. The relationship between the three rejected elements (vv21-23) moves inward: festivals → sacrifices → music, covering the entire ritual system.
24: But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream — ve-yiggal ka-mayim mishpat u-tsedaqah ke-nachal eitan: the counterpoint to vv21-23. Gal (roll, surge) — an active rolling force, not a trickle. Nachal eitan (ever-flowing stream) — eitan (perennial, constant, permanent) contrasts with the wadi that flows only seasonally; the justice YHWH demands must be permanent, not occasional. The pair mishpat (justice — procedural, judicial) and tsedaqah (righteousness — relational, covenant-faithful) is the Amos signature-phrase (also Amos 5:7; Amos 6:12); together they describe the full texture of covenant life that Israel has abandoned.
25: Did you bring me sacrifices during the forty years in the wilderness? — ha-zevachim u-minchah higgashtem li va-midbar arba'im shanah: the question is rhetorical with an implied answer of 'no' — the wilderness generation is idealized as a period before the corrupted cult of Canaan took root (cf. Jeremiah 7:21-23; Hosea 9:10). The point is not a literal denial of wilderness sacrifice (the Pentateuch records sacrifices) but a prophetic idealization: the covenant relationship with YHWH was primary; sacrifice was supposed to serve it, not replace it.
26: You carried your shrine of Sikkuth and Kiyyun — u-nesa'atem et Sikkut malkeykhem ve-et Kiyyun tsalmeykhom: Sikkuth and Kiyyun (or Chiun) are star-deities — probably Assyrian Sakkut and Kaiwanu (the planet Saturn). The MT vocalizes with the vowels of sheqets (detestable thing) to indicate contempt. Kochav eloheykhem (your star-god) — astral worship combined with YHWH worship. Stephen's speech in Acts 7:42-43 cites the LXX version of this verse, which names Rephan (for Kiyyun) and applies it to Israel's idolatry in the wilderness.
27: Therefore I will send you into exile beyond Damascus — ve-higleti etkhem me-halah le-Dammasheq: the judgment clause at the end of the oracle sequence. Exile beyond Damascus points northeast — into Assyria, which lies beyond Damascus. The deportation of the northern kingdom by Assyria (722 BC) fulfilled this directly (2 Kings 17:6). Amar YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot shemo (says the LORD, whose name is the God of armies) — the closing divine title grounds the judgment oracle in the one who controls armies and history.
